dialogue.utterance: Hi, I'm Alex, an EMT with the rescue squad. What made you call 911 today?
I've been feeling unwell and had visual hallucinations this morning.
I see. Are there any other symptoms you're experiencing right now?
Last night I began feeling worse, and this morning I couldn't see clearly and had hallucinations.
Can you tell me your name, where we are, and what day it is to help us assess your mental status?
My name is Mary. We're at my apartment in Richmond. The day is Monday.
Ma'am, we're going to take your blood pressure now.
Your blood pressure is 114 over 70.
Pulse is 86.
Respirations are 12.
Oxygen saturation is 96% on 3 L/min.
Blood glucose is 118 mg/dL.
EKG shows atrial fibrillation.
When did the hallucinations start, and were they constant or intermittent?
They started this morning and have stopped now, thank God.
Let me check your list of medications. You are currently taking metoclopramide, Xarelto, and allopurinol, among others. Is there any new medication you started?
No, I haven't changed my medications.
Have you been to the hospital recently?
Yes, I was at Chippenham last week for a UTI.
When was the last time you ate or drank anything?
Yesterday afternoon, I had some soup and tea.
What led up to the hallucinations and call for help?
I started seeing things that weren't there. My nephew called 911 after I got pretty scared.
You mentioned hallucinations. Is there anything that seems to trigger them?
I don't recall anything. I just began seeing shadows and shapes.
You are currently alert and oriented. Do you remember where you are and who I am?
Yes, you are the EMT, and we are at my apartment.
You have a history of COPD and CHF. Is that right?
Yes, I've managed those conditions for some time now.
Per standing orders, I attempted an IV at an extremity vein (arm or leg), but it was unsuccessful after one attempt.
We've started oxygen at 3 L/min via nasal cannula as per your home settings. Your oxygen saturation looks better.
I've placed a 4-lead ECG to monitor your rhythm.
The EKG shows atrial fibrillation with right bundle branch block, but no ST-segment elevations. I will now perform a 12-lead EKG.
We've obtained the 12-lead EKG. It confirms atrial fibrillation with right bundle branch block but no ST elevations.
Due to your size, we'll need assistance to get you to the ambulance. We have two police officers on site to help.
Let me retake your vitals. Your respirations have increased to 20.
Pulse is now 90, Blood pressure is lower at 86/45, O2 saturation 95% on 3 L.
Are you currently feeling any pain or discomfort?
No, not at all. The hallucinations have stopped now, and I feel better.
Another IV attempt was made, but it remains unsuccessful.
Let's go over your symptoms again. Did anything change with your visual hallucinations?
No, they've stopped altogether now.
I understand your nephew mentioned wetness on your pants. Is that correct?
Yes, that's from my skin oozing, and it has a foul smell.
I'll note this in the report. Any pain in your abdomen or pelvis?
No pain, just the wetness and smell.
Based on the EKG findings, vitals, and symptoms, we'll follow the pulmonary edema/CHF protocol for transport.
Do you have any pain now?
No, I currently feel good with no pain.
Great. During transport, we'll continue to monitor your breathing and heart rhythm.
We are transporting you to Chippenham Hospital for further evaluation and treatment.

The patient is a 62-year-old female with a psychiatric complaint secondary to transient visual hallucinations and feeling unwell. She is alert and oriented (A&Ox4). Initial vitals included BP 114/70, pulse 86, respirations 12, O2 saturation 96%, blood glucose 118. EKG showed atrial fibrillation with RBBB but no ST elevations. Later vitals included BP 86/45, then 94/47, with O2 sat 95% and 100% at different times. Pulse was 90 and 67. IV access was unsuccessful. Oxygen at 3 L/min via nasal cannula was administered as per home orders. Respirations increased to 20 and 24, respectively. She has a history of COPD and CHF. Transported to Chippenham for further care.
At the hospital, she rolled herself from the cot to the bed without incident.
Are you still feeling well now?
Yes, no pain and no new symptoms.
Additional EKG readings were ongoing in the ambulance to monitor for any changes in rhythm.
You are doing well. We'll keep checking your breathing and heart as we move out.
We will maintain close observation of the EKG and ensure no new arrhythmias begin.
Everything is in place for transport. You'll stay comfortable and monitored during the ride to the hospital.
You'll be at Chippenham for further monitoring and evaluation after we arrive.
Thanks for helping me out.
Of course, you're safe now. We're here to make sure you get the help you need.
I can confirm again that she has no pain right now, no new symptoms, and appears stable.
All systems are green for transport now. We'll be rolling you on out shortly.
